title: A World in Crisis
theme: Hope, Complexity & the Capacity for Change

paragraphs.en: We are living, by almost any measure, in a period of unusually compounded difficulty. Climate breakdown accelerating; democratic institutions under strain; inequality widening; trust in nearly every major institution in decline; the pace of technological change outrunning our collective capacity to govern it. It would be easy — and in some circles fashionable — to conclude from this that civilisation is in terminal decline, that the problems are too large and too interconnected to be solved, and that the appropriate response is either despair or a particular kind of aggressive nihilism dressed up as realism.
paragraphs.fr: Nous vivons, selon presque toutes les mesures, dans une période de difficulté inhabituellement composée. L'effondrement climatique s'accélère ; les institutions démocratiques sont sous pression ; les inégalités se creusent ; la confiance dans presque toutes les grandes institutions est en déclin ; le rythme du changement technologique dépasse notre capacité collective à le gouverner. Il serait facile — et dans certains cercles à la mode — de conclure de tout cela que la civilisation est en déclin terminal, que les problèmes sont trop vastes et trop interconnectés pour être résolus, et que la réponse appropriée est soit le désespoir, soit un nihilisme agressif particulier déguisé en réalisme.

paragraphs.en: I want to resist that conclusion — not from optimism, which is a temperamental disposition, but from evidence. The world is full of things that have not improved, and I don't intend to minimise them. But the historical record is also full of things that were considered intractable and turned out not to be: the end of colonial empires; the abolition of slavery; the provision of universal education and healthcare in dozens of countries; the dramatic reduction in extreme poverty over the past forty years; the near-elimination of smallpox and the significant reduction of several other diseases. None of these happened automatically or inevitably. All required sustained effort, political will, and the willingness of large numbers of people to act on a belief that things could be different.
paragraphs.fr: Je veux résister à cette conclusion — non pas par optimisme, qui est une disposition tempéramentale, mais par les preuves. Le monde est plein de choses qui ne se sont pas améliorées, et je n'ai pas l'intention de les minimiser. Mais le bilan historique est aussi plein de choses qui étaient considérées comme inextricables et qui n'ont finalement pas été : la fin des empires coloniaux ; l'abolition de l'esclavage ; la mise en place de l'éducation et des soins de santé universels dans des dizaines de pays ; la réduction dramatique de la pauvreté extrême au cours des quarante dernières années ; l'quasi-élimination de la variole et la réduction significative de plusieurs autres maladies. Rien de tout cela ne s'est produit automatiquement ou inévitablement. Tout a nécessité des efforts soutenus, de la volonté politique et la volonté d'un grand nombre de personnes d'agir sur la conviction que les choses pouvaient être différentes.

paragraphs.en: What history also shows is that change rarely comes from the direction that seemed most likely. The civil rights movement emerged from the most oppressed community in American society, not from the halls of power. The end of apartheid happened faster than almost anyone had predicted, and through a negotiated settlement that was considered impossible until it wasn't. The most transformative social changes of the last century often looked impossible right up until they happened, and then seemed inevitable in retrospect.
paragraphs.fr: Ce que l'histoire montre aussi, c'est que le changement vient rarement de la direction qui semblait la plus probable. Le mouvement des droits civiques est émergé de la communauté la plus opprimée de la société américaine, pas des couloirs du pouvoir. La fin de l'apartheid s'est produite plus vite que presque tout le monde ne l'avait prédit, et par un accord négocié qui était considéré impossible jusqu'à ce qu'il ne le soit plus. Les changements sociaux les plus transformateurs du siècle dernier semblaient souvent impossibles jusqu'à ce qu'ils se produisent, puis semblaient inévitables avec le recul.

paragraphs.en: This does not mean that positive change is guaranteed, or that we should be complacent about the scale of the problems. It means that the relationship between what seems possible and what actually is possible is more complex than our forecasting ability tends to allow for. The future is genuinely open in ways that both pessimists and optimists consistently underestimate.
paragraphs.fr: Cela ne signifie pas que le changement positif est garanti, ou que nous devrions être complaisants face à l'ampleur des problèmes. Cela signifie que la relation entre ce qui semble possible et ce qui est réellement possible est plus complexe que notre capacité de prévision ne tend à le permettre. L'avenir est véritablement ouvert d'une façon que les pessimistes et les optimistes sous-estiment systématiquement.

paragraphs.en: What strikes me most about the people doing serious work on the hardest problems is not that they are optimists. Many of them are not. What they share is something more like defiant hope — a conviction that it is worth trying to improve things even without confidence that the effort will succeed, because the alternative, which is not trying, is certainly worse. This is a more durable foundation for action than optimism, because it doesn't require good news to sustain it.
paragraphs.fr: Ce qui me frappe le plus chez les personnes qui travaillent sérieusement sur les problèmes les plus difficiles n'est pas qu'elles sont optimistes. Beaucoup d'entre elles ne le sont pas. Ce qu'elles partagent, c'est quelque chose de plus proche d'un espoir défiant — la conviction qu'il vaut la peine d'essayer d'améliorer les choses même sans avoir la certitude que l'effort réussira, parce que l'alternative, qui est de ne pas essayer, est certainement pire. C'est un fondement d'action plus durable que l'optimisme, parce qu'il ne nécessite pas de bonnes nouvelles pour le soutenir.

paragraphs.en: The course you have just completed has been, in part, an exercise in this kind of thinking. The issues it has engaged with — inequality, democracy, justice, identity, technology, consciousness, language — are not problems with solutions. They are perennial features of the human condition that require ongoing navigation, revision, and honest engagement. The English language is one of the most powerful tools available for that navigation: for expressing nuance, for examining assumptions, for arguing with precision, for finding common ground across difference. Use it well.
paragraphs.fr: Le cours que vous venez de terminer a été, en partie, un exercice dans ce genre de pensée. Les sujets qu'il a abordés — l'inégalité, la démocratie, la justice, l'identité, la technologie, la conscience, le langage — ne sont pas des problèmes ayant des solutions. Ce sont des caractéristiques pérennes de la condition humaine qui nécessitent une navigation continue, une révision et un engagement honnête. La langue anglaise est l'un des outils les plus puissants disponibles pour cette navigation : pour exprimer la nuance, pour examiner les présupposés, pour argumenter avec précision, pour trouver un terrain commun à travers les différences. Utilisez-la bien.

vocabulary.definition_en: composé de multiples éléments combinés ; dans les contextes de crise, plusieurs problèmes se renforçant mutuellement
vocabulary.example: The crisis is compounded — economic, political and environmental problems are mutually reinforcing.

vocabulary.definition_en: le rejet de tout sens, de toute moralité ou de toute possibilité de changement positif
vocabulary.example: Aggressive nihilism dressed as realism is a tempting but dangerous intellectual posture.

vocabulary.definition_en: trop difficile à traiter ; qui ne se gère ou ne se contrôle pas facilement
vocabulary.example: Many problems that once seemed intractable were eventually addressed through sustained effort.

vocabulary.definition_en: la fin formelle d'une pratique ou d'une institution, en particulier l'esclavage
vocabulary.example: The abolition of slavery required decades of political struggle and moral argument.

vocabulary.definition_en: une maladie très contagieuse et mortelle, déclarée éradiquée par l'OMS en 1980 — la seule maladie humaine à avoir été éradiquée
vocabulary.example: The eradication of smallpox is one of the greatest achievements of international cooperation.

vocabulary.definition_en: en regardant en arrière sur les événements ; en repensant au passé
vocabulary.example: In retrospect, the changes that seemed impossible seemed inevitable.

vocabulary.definition_en: qui montre une satisfaction non critique envers le statu quo ; qui ne voit pas ou ne traite pas les problèmes
vocabulary.definition_fr: complaisant, satisfait sans raison, non vigilant
vocabulary.example: Acknowledging progress does not mean being complacent about remaining problems.

vocabulary.definition_en: un espoir qui persiste face aux preuves contraires, refusant d'accepter l'impossibilité du changement
vocabulary.example: Defiant hope is more durable than optimism because it doesn't require good news.

vocabulary.definition_en: qui dure ou réapparaît sans cesse ; persistant au fil des années
vocabulary.example: Justice is a perennial question, not a problem to be solved once and set aside.

vocabulary.definition_en: réduire quelque chose au minimum possible ; présenter comme moins important que ça ne l'est
vocabulary.example: I don't intend to minimise the scale of the problem.

grammar_points.title: 'Not from X, which is Y, but from Z' — grounding a claim in evidence rather than feeling
grammar_points.explanation: Distinguer entre **une disposition ressentie** (optimisme, pessimisme) et **une position fondée sur les preuves** est un mouvement argumentatif sophistiqué. La formule « not from X (tempérament) but from Z (preuves) » signale la rigueur intellectuelle.
examples: 'I resist that conclusion — **not from optimism**, which is a temperamental disposition, **but from evidence**.'
'My confidence comes **not from certainty** — I have none — **but from** the historical record.'
'**Not because** it's easy, **but because** it's the only honest response to the evidence.'
This framing makes your conclusion more compelling because it is grounded, not merely felt.

grammar_points.title: 'Seemed impossible right up until they happened, then seemed inevitable in retrospect'
grammar_points.explanation: Le **contraste avant/après** dans l'argument historique est une structure puissante pour remettre en question l'hypothèse que les conditions actuelles sont permanentes. Le paradoxe « impossible → inévitable » est mémorable et pointe les limites de notre capacité de prévision.
examples: 'They **seemed impossible** right up until they happened, then **seemed inevitable** in retrospect.'
'The outcome appeared unthinkable in advance and obvious in hindsight.'
'What looked like a wall **turned out** to be a door, once someone tried the handle.'
This structure is particularly effective in arguing against fatalism.

grammar_points.title: The conclusive address: 'Use it well' — the imperative as closing gesture
grammar_points.explanation: Terminer un long argument par un **impératif** court et direct crée un effet de finalité et un appel à l'action. Après une analyse complexe, la simplicité d'un ordre direct signale que le temps de la réflexion est terminé et que celui de l'action a commencé.
examples: A short imperative ending is a classical rhetorical device. It should feel earned rather than preachy.

expressions.meaning_fr: déguisé en réalisme, se faisant passer pour du réalisme
expressions.example: Nihilism dressed up as realism is a tempting but ultimately dishonest intellectual posture.
expressions.note: Utilisé pour décrire une position qui emploie le langage du pragmatisme intransigeant pour justifier l'inaction ou le désespoir. « Dressed up as » implique un déguisement.

expressions.meaning_fr: considéré impossible jusqu'à ce qu'il ne le soit plus
expressions.example: The settlement was considered impossible until it wasn't.
expressions.note: Une formulation compacte et élégante pour l'idée que l'impossibilité perçue est historiquement contingente, pas permanente.

expressions.meaning_fr: espoir défiant, espoir obstiné face aux obstacles
expressions.example: What the most serious workers share is defiant hope — not optimism.
expressions.note: Un concept qui sépare l'espoir de l'optimisme. L'optimisme nécessite de bonnes nouvelles ; l'espoir défiant persiste sans elles, ancré dans la valeur de l'effort plutôt que dans la probabilité de succès.

expressions.meaning_fr: navigation continue, travail de navigation permanent
expressions.example: Perennial human questions require ongoing navigation, not one-time solutions.
expressions.note: Une métaphore de navigation pour l'idée que certains problèmes nécessitent un pilotage constant plutôt qu'une destination à atteindre puis à laisser derrière soi.

expressions.example: Language is a tool for finding common ground across difference.
expressions.note: Un concept central de la théorie politique pluraliste et de la communication. « Common ground » ne signifie pas un accord sur tout — c'est un espace partagé à partir duquel un désaccord productif peut opérer.

written.question: On what basis does the author resist a pessimistic conclusion?
options: On the basis of personal optimism about human nature.
On the basis of the historical evidence of previously intractable problems being resolved.
On the basis of current data showing improvement in key indicators.
On the basis of scientific projections about future technological capacity.
written.explanation: 'I want to resist that conclusion — not from optimism, which is a temperamental disposition, but from evidence... the historical record is full of things that were considered intractable and turned out not to be.'

written.question: What does the author mean by 'defiant hope'?
options: Hope based on confident predictions of a positive future.
A conviction that trying to improve things is worthwhile even without certainty of success.
The refusal to acknowledge the scale of current problems.
Hope that is resistant to factual correction.
written.explanation: 'A conviction that it is worth trying to improve things even without confidence that the effort will succeed, because the alternative, which is not trying, is certainly worse.'

written.sentence: I argue this ________ ________ optimism — I have no particular reason to expect good news — ________ ________ the historical record.
written.hint: The structure that grounds a claim in evidence rather than temperament.

written.sentence: The change ________ impossible right up until it happened, then ________ inevitable with hindsight.
written.hint: The before/after historical contrast — the same verb, two opposite perspectives.

written.sentence: These are ________ features of human life that require continuous engagement, not problems to be solved once.

written.french: Je veux résister à cette conclusion — non pas par optimisme, mais par les preuves que des problèmes considérés insolubles ont déjà été résolus.
written.suggested_answer: I want to resist that conclusion — not from optimism, but from the evidence that problems considered intractable have been resolved before.
key_vocabulary: not from optimism but from evidence

written.french: L'espoir défiant est plus durable que l'optimisme, car il ne nécessite pas de bonnes nouvelles pour être soutenu.
written.suggested_answer: Defiant hope is more durable than optimism, because it doesn't require good news to sustain it.
key_vocabulary: doesn't require good news to sustain it

written.french: Les changements les plus transformateurs semblaient impossibles jusqu'à ce qu'ils se produisent, puis semblaient inévitables avec le recul.
written.suggested_answer: The most transformative changes seemed impossible right up until they happened, then seemed inevitable in retrospect.
key_vocabulary: impossible right up until they happened

oral.instruction: Read the final paragraph of the course — it is addressed directly to the learner. Read it with warmth, sincerity and a sense of occasion.
oral.text: The course you have just completed has been, in part, an exercise in this kind of thinking. The issues it has engaged with — inequality, democracy, justice, identity, technology, consciousness, language — are not problems with solutions. They are perennial features of the human condition that require ongoing navigation, revision, and honest engagement. The English language is one of the most powerful tools available for that navigation: for expressing nuance, for examining assumptions, for arguing with precision, for finding common ground across difference. Use it well.
oral.focus: The list of issues should feel like a recognition of shared territory — you and the reader have explored these together. The final sentence — 'Use it well' — should be simple, direct, and complete.

oral.question: Looking back over this course, which lesson or idea has stayed with you most strongly? What did it change or deepen in how you think?
oral.suggested_answer: The idea that has stayed with me most is from the lesson on language — Wittgenstein's claim that the limits of your language are the limits of your world. I understood intellectually what it meant before I started this course, but working through the language itself — the precise vocabulary of argument, the specific structures of nuance and concession — has given me a felt sense of what it means. When I can name something more precisely in English, I actually think about it more precisely. That's a claim about thought, not just vocabulary. What it's deepened is my appreciation for the work involved in genuinely understanding something — not just recognising it, but being able to move around inside it, argue from it, qualify it, push back on it.
vocabulary_hints: felt sense of what it means
argue from it, qualify it, push back on it

oral.question: What gives you 'defiant hope' — not blind optimism, but a conviction that it is worth trying? Make a personal, argued response.
oral.suggested_answer: What gives me defiant hope is something the course has touched on several times without quite naming directly: the gap between what seems permanent and what is. The things that seem most fixed — political structures, cultural norms, the distribution of power — are the ones with the most remarkable history of change. Apartheid seemed permanent. The European wars seemed permanent. Child labour seemed permanent. None of them were. That doesn't mean every problem resolves, and it doesn't mean resolution happens without cost. But it does mean that 'this is how things are' is never the same as 'this is how things must be'. For me, that distinction — and the English to articulate it precisely — is enough to keep working.
vocabulary_hints: the gap between what seems permanent and what is
the most remarkable history of change
'this is how things are' is never the same as 'this is how things must be'
